questions.prompt: Why use custom bridge networks instead of the default bridge?

choices.text: Built-in DNS. The default bridge network doesn't resolve container names — containers must use IP addresses to reach each other. Custom bridge networks include Docker's embedded DNS resolver (at 127.0.0.11), so containers resolve each other by container name. `api`, `db`, `redis` all just work. Docker Compose creates a custom bridge automatically. Use custom bridges for any multi-container setup; reserve default bridge for one-off containers.

questions.explanation: Custom bridges have DNS; default doesn't. Use custom bridges for any multi-container setup.

questions.prompt: An NGINX container running on host network can't bind to port 80 because another service has it. Fix?

choices.text: Switch to bridge networking with port publishing. With `--network host`, the container shares the host's network namespace — no isolation, no port mapping, port conflicts with host services. With bridge networking and `-p 8080:80`, NGINX binds to port 80 inside its own namespace; Docker maps host port 8080 to it. No conflict with the other service on host port 80. Bridge + port publishing is the standard pattern; host networking is for specific performance or isolation-not-needed cases.

questions.explanation: Host networking shares host's net namespace = port conflicts. Bridge + port publish isolates port spaces.
